_about: PRE-FILLED SAMPLE — Por Thozhil (Vignesh Raja, 2023). Import this into the Filmmaker's Blueprint to see what a fully filled project looks like. Curated by Arunak. To use: open the blueprint, click IMPORT, select this file. Your existing work will be replaced — export it first if you want to keep it.
meta_started: Sample data — based on the 2023 film
s1_whatif: What if a serial killer who stopped after 1979 starts killing again — and a faint-hearted, book-smart rookie has to partner with a reclusive veteran cop to stop him before more young women die?
s1_why_me: I want to write a Tamil thriller that refuses every easy commercial trick — no songs, no comedy track, no hero entry. A film that proves a procedural can be cinematic without being flashy.
s1_image: A police station at 2 AM, fluorescent lights, two cops studying a 1979 case file with the dust still on it. The younger one is sweating; the older one is calm.
s2_log1: When a serial killer starts again decades later, a rookie must catch him.
s2_log2: When a serial killer who stopped in 1979 starts killing again, a rookie cop must overcome his fear and partner with a reclusive veteran to catch him.
s2_log_final: When a serial killer who stopped after 1979 starts killing again, a faint-hearted rookie cop must overcome his fear and partner with a reclusive veteran to catch the killer before more young women die.
s3_ext: More young women will be killed if the cops fail. The killer has been operating undetected for decades and will continue.
s3_int: The rookie's belief that he is not strong enough to be a real cop will be confirmed; the veteran's reclusive grief over an old failure will harden into permanent retreat.
s3_phil: If they fail, the idea that empathy and patience can crack a case — that procedure is more powerful than force — gets disproven.
s4_age: 28, IPS rookie, on his first major posting
s4_want: To do well in his first big case so he can prove himself.
s4_need: To stop being a cop because his family wants it and accept (or quit) on his own terms.
s4_lie: Wearing the uniform is enough to be a cop.
s4_wound: Family pressure to enter the IPS; never chose this himself.
s4_skill: Book-smart. Photographic memory for case detail. Methodical reader of files.
s4_flaw: Physically untrained, panics under physical threat, freezes at gore.
s4_arc: By the final scene, he has chosen this work for the first time — and made his own terms with what kind of cop he can be.
s5_want: To return to the only thing that has ever made him feel powerful — killing — after decades of suppressed routine life.
s5_philosophy: He has a coherent inner logic rooted in childhood trauma and a specific psychological pattern; the film treats it as a puzzle to be assembled, not a label to be slapped on.
s5_hero: He sees himself as someone reclaiming a self the world forced him to bury. In his interior life, the killings are an act of becoming whole again.
s5_mirror: He mirrors what unspent trauma can do over decades — what Loganathan and Prakash both risk if they don't process their own.
s5_humanity: His domestic life — meek, gentle, unremarkable — humanises him for one chilling scene. The audience almost understands.
s6_ally_reflect: He shows Prakash what cop work actually is — patience, file-reading, motive-hunting — versus the heroic version Prakash imagined.
s6_ally_subplot: Loganathan is haunted by an old failed case from his youth; the new investigation is also a return to that ghost.
s6_ally_exit: He doesn't leave — but he hands Prakash the lead at the climax, a quiet abdication of authority that completes Prakash's arc.
s6_lover_name: (none — film deliberately has no romantic subplot)
s6_mentor_tool: The principle: motive matters more than evidence. Investigate the why; the who follows.
s6_mentor_failure: The 1979 case he never solved as a young officer.
s7_location: Trichy and surrounding small-town Tamil Nadu
s7_duration: Roughly two weeks of investigation, intercut with 1979
s7_rules: 1. No songs. 2. Mostly night. 3. No comedy track. 4. No romantic subplot. 5. No hero introduction shot.
s7_sensory: Sodium-lamp orange, fluorescent green, file-dust beige. Sound: rain, distant traffic, fluorescent buzz, the rustle of paper. Recurring object: the 1979 case file itself.
b01: Loganathan in his sparse home, alone with old files. World before disturbance.
b02: An older cop says 'motive ah paaru, evidence ah illa' — the principle that will become Prakash's lesson.
b03: Prakash arrives, eager and untested. The station shown as procedural, real, unglamorous.
b04: A young woman is found murdered in a way that matches a 1979 file. Loganathan is summoned out of retreat.
b05: Prakash debates whether he can handle this case. He nearly asks for reassignment.
b06: The rookie commits — partnering with Loganathan officially. New rules: no shortcuts, follow process.
b07: B-story: Loganathan's own grief over the 1979 case begins surfacing through Prakash's questions.
b08: The investigation: file-reading, interviews, location work. The 'fun and games' is the procedural — promise of the premise.
b09: Midpoint: a suspect emerges. Looks open-and-shut. False victory.
b10: The suspect's profile is wrong. The real killer is closer than they thought, and another body appears.
b11: A near-miss with the killer, where Prakash freezes physically. All his fears confirmed. Worst night of his life.
b12: Prakash alone, considers quitting. Loganathan also at his lowest — confronting his old failure.
b13: Prakash returns with a new approach — using the 'motive' principle to look at the killer's psychology, not his trail.
b14: They identify the killer through psychological deduction. Confrontation. Procedural, not heroic.
b15: Final image: Prakash quieter, older. Loganathan having handed over the work. The file closes — but the world remains.
s9_chrono:
1. 1979 — original killings
2. Decades of dormancy
3. Recent killing — case 1
4. Loganathan summoned
5. Prakash assigned
6. Investigation begins
7. Wrong suspect
8. Real killer pattern emerges
9. Climactic confrontation
10. Aftermath
s9_screen:
1. Recent killing
2. Loganathan / Prakash meet
3. Glimpses of 1979 woven in
4. Investigation
5. 1979 reframed at midpoint
6. Real killer revealed
7. Confrontation
8. Quiet ending
s9_why: The 1979 footage is parcelled out so each fragment recontextualises a present-day scene. By the time the audience sees the full 1979 picture, they are also at the climax of the present-day case. Two timelines converge.
s10_setup_1: Two cops chat casually about one's wife — looks like throwaway comedy. ~p15
s10_pay_1: Forty minutes later, that conversation is mirrored in a different character's actual behaviour — reframing the casual scene as a planted clue. ~p55
s10_setup_2: Loganathan's hand trembles holding a file early on. Played as old-age detail. ~p20
s10_pay_2: Same tremor returns at the climax — but now we know it's not age, it's the 1979 case. ~p115
s10_setup_3: A railway-crossing detail mentioned in passing during a witness interview. ~p35
s10_pay_3: The crossing becomes critical to identifying the killer's movement pattern. ~p100
s10_setup_4: Prakash's photographic memory shown when he reads a file in front of Loganathan. ~p25
s10_pay_4: That same memory is what cracks the case in the third act. ~p110
s10_setup_5: A child plays a specific game in the background of a 1979 flashback. ~p30
s10_pay_5: The game returns as a behaviour pattern of the killer. ~p105
sl_2_want: Read the body before forensics arrive
sl_3_conf: Files are disordered, decades of neglect
sl_4_want: Refuse the case and return home
sl_5_want: Witness statement from a casual chai stall vendor
sl_5_conf: Vendor is evasive; Prakash too polite
s12_note_v1: When you're 60 pages in: the principle is 'motive over evidence.' If a scene does not advance motive understanding, cut it.
v2s1_draft: Final draft locked. ~135 pages. ~110 scenes. Estimated runtime ~145 minutes.
v2s1_weak: 1. The 1979 fragments must read clearly — risk of audience confusion. 2. The 'no songs' decision means every silence must be earned. 3. The killer's reveal hinges on payoff timing — if too early, predictable; if too late, frustrating.
v2s1_signoff: Producer, lead cast (Sarathkumar, Ashok Selvan), DOP (Kalaiselvan Sivaji), 1st AD
v2s2_theme: Motive matters more than evidence — and patience matters more than force.
v2s2_refs: Zodiac (Fincher, 2007); Memories of Murder (Bong, 2003); a non-film reference: real Tamil Nadu police case files from the 1970s-80s as a procedural texture source.
v2s2_refuse: No songs. No comedy track. No romantic subplot. No mass-hero entry. No daytime ambience. No gore for shock value. No flashy camera moves. No genre-typical jump scares.
v2s2_para: Por Thozhil is a Tamil procedural thriller about a 1979 case that comes back to life. It is quiet, mostly night, refuses songs, and is built on the premise that motive — psychology, biography — matters more than evidence. The texture is real police stations, real files, real Trichy locations. Every department's job is to make the audience feel that this case is being solved by patience, not by force. We refuse glamour, we refuse mass, we refuse heroism. The film must read like a procedural, look like a quiet horror, and end like an elegy.
v2s3_palette: Sodium-lamp orange, fluorescent green, file-dust beige.
v2s3_light: Films: Zodiac, Memories of Murder, Prisoners (Villeneuve). Photographer: Greg Williams' on-set work. Painter: Edward Hopper for the lonely-light interiors.
v2s3_frames:
1. Hopper's Nighthawks — for the police-station-at-night feel.
2. Zodiac's basement scene — for procedural dread.
3. Memories of Murder rural-night frames.
4. Prisoners' garage scenes — for confined-space tension.
5. Real cold-case file photography — for grain texture.
6. Tamil Nadu police archive photography — for period authenticity.
v2s3_location: Folder: /preprod/lookbook/ — shared with DOP, AD, costume, art.
shot_1_desc: Loganathan walks into archive room — alone with files
shot_1_notes: Single fluorescent practical, cold green tint. Hold long.
shot_3_notes: Dawn light, cold blue. No sting.
shot_4_desc: Two cops chat casually about wife (setup #1)
shot_5_desc: Prakash freezes during near-miss with killer
shot_5_notes: His POV bleeds in and out. Sound design carries it.
v2s5_lens: Cooke S4 Mini set, with selected vintage Lomo for night exteriors
v2s5_speciallens: Probe lens for one file insert
v2s5_light_phil: Always one motivated source. Practicals over fixtures. Sodium and fluorescent are the dominant colour temperatures.
v2s5_grade: Kodak Vision3 250D feel; muted desaturated grade with green-amber lift in shadows.
v2s6_sets: Police station interior (built for control). Loganathan's house interior (real location dressed). 1979 archive room.
v2s6_dressed: Trichy police station exterior. Railway crossing. Several real Trichy streets. Suspect's house.
v2s6_heroprops: The 1979 case file (custom-built, aged). The killer's specific tool/object (deliberately ordinary).
v2s6_recurring: The case file (gathers meaning across scenes). Prakash's pen and notepad. Loganathan's reading glasses.
v2s6_period: 1979 flashback elements: police uniforms, documentation paperwork, telephones, vehicles. Also archive design.
v2s7_protag: Prakash starts in pressed crisp uniform with shoes shined — clearly trying. By midpoint, sleeves unrolled, wrinkles visible. By final scenes, comfortable in his own clothes — the uniform fits him, not the other way around.
v2s7_antag: The killer wears unremarkable, almost forgettable clothing throughout — by design. The audience should not be able to recall his outfit after seeing him. His domestic clothes are gentle and soft.
v2s7_period: 1979 flashback: police uniform style of the era; cotton bush shirts; specific bell-bottom cuts.
cast_1_notes: Cast against type — a quiet veteran rather than mass-hero swagger.
cast_2_notes: Cast against type — geeky, not imposing. The casting is part of the directorial argument.
cast_3_notes: Domestic, meek, unremarkable in his everyday scenes.
cast_4_notes: Provides one of the casual conversations that pays off later.
loc_1_name: Trichy Central Police Station — used for exteriors
v2s10_bgm: Tighten the scene; never decorate. Atmospheric layers; minimal melody; let silence play.
v2s10_refs: Zodiac (David Shire), Memories of Murder (Taro Iwashiro), Prisoners (Jóhann Jóhannsson).
v2s10_phil: Quiet film. Silence is a tool. Rooms must sound real. Fluorescent buzz, distant traffic, rain, fans — these carry the dread. The score holds back.
v2s11_expday: Climactic confrontation night — multiple cast, special lighting setup, controlled physical action.
v2s11_largest: Camera + lens package across ~50 days; cinematographer fees.
v2s12_recce:
Aug — Trichy Central Station + warehouse: Director, DOP, AD, Sound, PD
Aug — Railway crossing at night: Director, DOP, Sound
Aug — Loganathan's house exterior: Director, DOP, PD
v2s12_problems: 1. Sodium-lamp tint at the railway crossing varies by hour. 2. Warehouse acoustic — too live for the climax dialogue. 3. Police-station exterior had unexpected traffic noise after 10 PM. 4. One night-shoot location had a wedding hall nearby playing music until 11 PM. 5. Generator power requirement at remote location larger than expected.
v2s12_resolutions: 1. Lock specific shoot windows; gel as needed (Gaffer). 2. Treat warehouse with sound blankets; ADR-ready (Sound). 3. Permit-extend traffic block, reschedule scene to early morning (AD). 4. Negotiate with hall owner; alternate dates booked (PM). 5. Upgrade to 60 KVA gen (PM).
v2s12_note: When you're tired on day 18 and someone is asking the same question for the third time: the answer is in the Director's Vision. 'Motive over evidence. Patience over force.' If the choice on the floor doesn't serve those two principles, it's a bad choice — even if it looks easier.
